speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my family. To my far left is my beautiful granddaughter, Shoshana. [LAUGHTER] Next to her is my wonderful son-in-law, Paul Bentley. Next is my beautiful daughter, Sally. And next to Sally, to me, is my beautiful wife, Lily. That's me. And next to me, is my handsome guy, Tom.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son. And next is Jessica, the most beautiful girl in the world. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter. My granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you said, Jessica.

speaker: interviewee
text: No Oh. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um-- it's-- it's-- it's very interesting when you grow up, and you're a child of survivors, because you just assume that life is the way it is, and everything is quite normal. And the concept of grandparents is not one for you. Not having a very big extended family. Not having a family history is-- is something that you don't have, and you don't necessarily start to miss until you get older. And then you really wonder about what grandparents were like, and what would it to have been like to have grandparents. And that's why we're so-- we're so lucky that our children have four grandparents that are alive. And I can see, through my parents, and through my in-laws, the love, and the support, and the role modeling that your-- that grandchildren get. That's very different from what you get from parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I can't help tell my children how happy I am that they have that experience, because it's quite wonderful to be able to-- to learn from past generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my father and my mother have certainly shaped my political views. In a way that I don't think I would have had them be if I had been born in a different type of environment. And issues around our home, in terms of human rights and activism, are very profound. And I think it's because when you're a child of the-- of survivors, you're very sensitive to any human rights abuses within your own community and in the world around you. You know, if it can happen to the Jews, it can happen to anybody else. And we said, never again, to Jews. And it's also never again to anybody else. I think that's why in our family we have quite a history of political activism, and it is directly related to who my parents are-- who I love very much. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I would-- just to add, to echo that. And just to add a comment that because my parents were not survivors-- my parents are from England, and my father fought in the war in the English army. My mother didn't obviously get involved in the war in a direct way, but told me about her experiences. But they were so different from what I've heard from Dave and Lily. I've learned a great deal in a way that you can't possibly learn from books about what it was like in the war. What it was like in the camps. I mean, the stories that Dave has told us over the years-- it's just something that you just can't get out of a book. It's just an incomprehensible tale of how someone-- how people-- but Dave, in particular, was able to survive. I just can't imagine how I would have been able to survive what he survived during the war. And it's just a-- to me, it's a testament to some men's courage and some men's perseverance that he was able to do that for the years that he did and turned out the way he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's just a remarkable individual. So I just want to add to what Sally said. It's been a very unique experience having a father-in-law such as Dave and a mother-in-law such as Lily. Shoshana? Anything you wanted to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, well, when-- I know that Zadie was in the war, and I knew he was a Holocaust survival. And I know everybody keeps telling me how terrible it was and how awful it must have been, but I still can't image that it actually happened. And I know it did from reading all the books and everything I've heard about. It's just so terrible that it's hard to even imagine that somebody could think of doing something like that. And I want to believe that it's true, but I just can't imagine it at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the club named [NON-ENGLISH]. And it called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And this is on the field before we had we played a soccer game. The first, is the captain. His name was Nysek Wertheim. Then it comes the-- Nysek [? Wertheim ?] was [INAUDIBLE] in camp, and he got killed. He was a very, very nice, good guy. The second--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jacobs, are you going from left to right right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second is the goal keeper. His name was David Keinekait. The third was Ralph Gerstain. He is a survivor, and he lives in Toronto. The fourth is Weismann. He didn't survive. The fifth is Sam Szampaner. He lives. He survived, and lives in New York. Then is [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't survive. Then it comes me, David Jacobs. I am in Toronto. Then it comes Mendel Abe. He survived, and lives in Haifa, Israel. Then it comes Boguchwal. He didn't survive. Then it comes Szampaner. A brother from him. He lives in New York. And the last one is Gross. He didn't survive either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was roughly about 1937 before a soccer game.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomaszow. Beside that poles in the white t-shirt is my brother, the youngest. His name is [? Haskell. ?] He didn't survive. And then, I'm jumping. The high, it calls. The high jump. I happened to be very good in this. And beside to the two are two referees. One name is [? Scarupa. ?] He didn't survive. And the other is [? Vilamovski. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- that was taken on the stadium. There was a celebration. And one team did compete with the other in everything. In the high jumping, in running, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this in Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937. This is a picture taken before the game. We are standing in the goalie on the same [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] place. And his name-- my name is Jacobs. And my friend, Abe he survived. And he lives in Haifa, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you on the right or on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- what city was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the same year, like 1937 or 36, something like this. This is my friend, [? Hamer ?] [? Brunnholtz. ?] We worked together and we were very good friends. And he didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Tomaszow. And that was in the park. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on the left and who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I am on the left. And on the right is [? Brunnholtz. ?] This is a picture taken also in the park. On the left side, it's me. And beside me is Abe, what he lives in Haifa, Israel. That third is his name, [? Vinerot. ?] He did not survive. And the forth one is also [? Brunnholtz, ?] which he didn't survive either.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Tomaszow in the park. I would say approximately in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you members of the same organization, the four of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We did play in the same, this soccer team.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the organization that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]. But I-- but we played the soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was under the [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which in English could be translated as--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] no. The-- what we are together. I forgot how that's called. In Gymnastic Sport. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: So could one say, the Jewish Sports Club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Something like this. On the bottom row from left to right sits-- the first is Rolph Gerstain. He survived. He lives in Toronto. The second to him is me. The third is [INAUDIBLE]. He did not survive. The fourth is Abe, what I mentioned before. He lives in Haifa, Israel. On the top, is [? Govinski. ?] He did not survived. In the center is-- in the second [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he didn't survived. And the last one is [? Rosenblum. ?] He did not survive either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the my-- we took the like-- we took the like the cup, the great cup. The-- the-- from the whole city of Tomaszow. We-- we won the-- no. The great cup.

speaker: interviewer
text: The championship.

speaker: interviewee
text: The championship.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- what sport was it that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ping-pong. We played ping-pong.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this also affiliated with the Jewish sports club?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same club. The same club.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow. That was also 1936. And we took the championship in '37, too. And they were very good players. The team actually it go five people. There was one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in reserve. And when we went-- me, and Ralph, and Nysek. The three of us, we were sure we won the game. This is a document from the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was like a driver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I did help people, sick people to take to Sweden, Denmark, and re-transport them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year would this document have been issued to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it issued to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was issued in [GERMAN], the head quarters from the British zone in Germany. This is me. I was a driver for the American Joint Distribution Committee, like affiliated with the [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is I went in the car and went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you wearing a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was-- I had a permit to wear. I could wear a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to be selected to be a driver for the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody came up to you one day asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, that's right. Somebody came up, and asked me, and it happens they like me. This is the DP camp, Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there sitting on the left is [? Binya. ?] She lives in San Francisco. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then-- then is my brother, [? Noyich. ?] And then is my other cousin, [? Freinya. ?] She lives in Toronto. And then is my sister. She lives in Toronto. On top, in the second row is [? Shemin ?] [? Wold. ?] He's a brother to the-- to the [? Breinya ?] and [? Binya. ?] And then me in the uniform. And that is in Bergen-Belsen and near the block [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 11. 11, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. This is my brother to the left, and my sister, and me. This is the rest of the family what is left from nine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother on the far left. His full name is-- [? Noyich. ?] [? Noyich ?] Jacobs. And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister is [? Huma ?] [? Dichtenberg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was made in Bergen-Belsen. Near the round house, that they called their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the significance of the round house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before the war when the Germans there. That was like the officer's mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: A beautiful building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately, what year would this picture have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also 1947.
